title: Lipschitz rigidity for scalar curvature
speaker: Bernhard Hanke
affiliation: University of Augsburg, Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: Lower scalar curvature bounds on spin Riemannian manifolds exhibit remarkable rigidity properties determined by spectral properties of Dirac operators. For instance, a fundamental result of Llarull states that there is no smooth Riemannian metric on the n-sphere which dominates the round metric and whose scalar curvature is greater than or equal to the scalar curvature of the round metric, except the round metric itself. A similar result holds for smooth comparison maps from spin Riemannian manifolds to round spheres.

proof_stages.label: Scalar-curvature rigidity paradigm from the sphere
proof_stages.summary: After recalling the flexibility and obstructions in positive scalar curvature, the talk isolates rigidity and uses the two-sphere plus Gauss-Bonnet as the basic comparison model.

proof_stages.label: Smooth Llarull theorem and the spin-method limitation
proof_stages.summary: The classical higher-dimensional theorem is stated for closed connected spin manifolds mapping with nonzero degree to the round sphere, and the ensuing discussion explains why the known proof requires spin geometry.

proof_stages.label: Low-regularity formulation of the rigidity theorem
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines scalar curvature distributionally for W^{1,p} continuous metrics, states the low-regularity Llarull theorem for Lipschitz maps, and compares the Dirac approach with geometric-flow methods and the area-nonincreasing refinement.

proof_stages.label: Low-regularity twisted Dirac operator and index
proof_stages.summary: Using a smooth background spinor bundle and a Lipschitz pullback twist, the argument constructs a twisted Dirac operator that is essentially self-adjoint, Fredholm, parity-reversing, and still satisfies the Atiyah-Singer index formula.

proof_stages.label: Integrated Lichnerowicz formula
proof_stages.summary: An integral version of the Lichnerowicz formula is established for H^1 spinor sections, with the scalar term interpreted distributionally and the twist curvature obtained by pulling back smooth target curvature through the Lipschitz map.

proof_stages.label: Harmonic spinor equality case
proof_stages.summary: For the pullback of the sphere spinor bundle, the nonzero index gives a kernel spinor, and the Lichnerowicz inequality forces equality: constant spinor norm, sharp scalar curvature, and the differential of the map is an isometry almost everywhere.

proof_stages.label: Upgrade from a.e. isometry to metric isometry
proof_stages.summary: Since an almost-everywhere differential isometry can fold, the proof invokes quasiregular mapping theory for local homeomorphism and then uses the Clifford-involution equality condition to prove the needed orientation preservation almost everywhere.

proof_stages.label: Hemisphere rigidity by doubling
proof_stages.summary: As an application, a smooth hemisphere comparison theorem with mean-convex boundary is proved by doubling the domain to create a low-regularity closed metric and applying the already-proved low-regularity sphere theorem.

examples.name: Gauss-Bonnet rigidity on the two-sphere
examples.note: The round two-sphere with a metric satisfying scalar curvature at least 2 and dominating the round metric shows, via the fixed Gauss-Bonnet integral, the simplest version of scalar-curvature rigidity.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker proves the two-sphere rigidity by comparing the Gauss-Bonnet integrals and deriving a contradiction if the metric is strictly larger

examples.name: Pullback by a nonsmooth metric isometry
examples.note: Pulling back a smooth metric along a low-regularity map illustrates why the low-regularity theorem can only conclude metric isometry, not smoothness of the map.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs the pullback construction along a low-regularity map to explain why the theorem can only conclude metric isometry, not smoothness

examples.name: Fold map of the sphere
examples.note: The map that keeps one hemisphere fixed and flips the other is Lipschitz with differential an isometry almost everywhere but is not a metric isometry, forcing the extra orientation-preservation/local-homeomorphism ingredient.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly describes the fold map hemisphere by hemisphere and checks it is one-Lipschitz and an a.e. isometry but not a metric isometry

passes.reason: the fold collapses two hemispheres onto one and is a 1-Lipschitz a.e. isometry used to refute the naive metric-isometry conclusion
the fold map identifies the two hemispheres while saturating the almost-everywhere isometry condition, refuting that this alone gives a metric isometry
the fold is a collapsed piecewise isometry used to refute that an a.e. isometric differential forces a metric isometry
the sphere fold has differential an isometry almost everywhere yet globally folds/collapses orientation, so it refutes a.e.-isometry implying metric isometry
The fold identifies hemispheres while remaining a 1-Lipschitz a.e. isometry, so it is a collapsed tight case countering a metric-isometry conclusion from a.e. isometry alone.
The fold map is locally isometric almost everywhere but folds two hemispheres together, showing that this condition alone need not give a metric isometry.
the fold map has isometric differential almost everywhere but folds two hemispheres together, refuting that this alone implies a metric isometry.
The fold map has differential an isometry almost everywhere while folding two hemispheres together, so it refutes the naive metric-isometry conclusion.
the fold identifies two hemispheres while having isometric differential almost everywhere, refuting that this local condition alone gives a metric isometry
the fold map is a tight a.e.-isometric one-Lipschitz map that identifies hemispheres, refuting the implication from a.e. isometry to metric isometry
given as an explicit counterexample to a.e.-isometry implying metric isometry; it is a sharp piecewise isometric fold identifying two hemispheres.
The fold map shows that an a.e. isometric differential need not give a metric isometry, and it folds two hemispherical pieces while saturating the 1-Lipschitz/a.e.-isometry bound.

examples.name: Angle-multiplication branched quasiregular map
examples.note: The polar-coordinate map multiplying the angular variable by k illustrates branching in quasiregular mapping theory and why small distortion is needed to obtain local homeomorphisms.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs the polar angle-multiplication map, describes the wrapping and branching behavior, and states its quasiregular distortion
